findings.description: pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis (an untrusted/network boundary) allows arbitrary code execution via crafted pickle payloads (cache poisoning -> RCE); CWE-502, OWASP A08:2021. Fix: use JSON/msgpack, or HMAC-sign payloads and verify before unpickling, or a find_class allowlist Unpickler.

_is_expired returns 'age < self.ttl', inverting the TTL check so the L1 cache serves entries PAST their TTL and discards fresh ones; security angle is that revoked/sensitive values cached past their intended expiry keep being returned, defeating TTL-based invalidation (primary correctness bug is owned by the logic critic).

self._local dict has no size cap or eviction policy and (compounded by the broken expiry check) grows unbounded; if cache keys are derived from user input via set/get_or_compute/warm, an attacker can exhaust process memory (DoS); CWE-770 / CWE-400.

Values are written to the L2 Redis backend as plaintext pickled bytes with no application-layer encryption and the client shows no auth/TLS; if sensitive data (tokens/PII) is cached it is exposed at rest on Redis (deployment/config concern, only partly determinable from this module).
